text: The proposal of this video is to present the ureco-reminent capillary abnormalities with a robotic treatment and partial cystectomy under the TENT technique. The ureco is a fibrosis remnant of the allantoids, which form the median umbilical ligament as the ceiling occurs. Ureco remnant can be found during surgical treatment of infiltrate endometriosis and other surgical gynecologic pathologies. Some abnormalities can occur with the sealing of the channel during the fetal development, leading to symptoms such as umbilical drainage, chronic pelvic pain and urinary tract infections.
And there are at least four specific types of uricle abnormalities. The patent uricles, uricle sinus, diverticulum and uricle cysts.

Using the fourth arm technique, we initiate the robotic procedure. and emphasizing that when associated with other surgical pelvic disease should be the last one performed, like in this case after myomectomy.
The surgeon exposes the reticulospase and urechial pattern for safe resection, filling the bladder with 300 cc of methylene blue solution to enhance anatomical visibility.
The surgeon adjusts the optic to a 30-degree upward angle to enhance anatomical exposure for evaluating and treating the uricose remnant, facilitating a safe resection.

The surgeon performs a resection of the urocal patent associated with diverticulectomy and apical cystectomy, ensuring the bladder mucosa remains intact. The procedure is facilitated by stable imaging and strategic port placement, contributing to improved recovery by avoiding the use of a bladder catheter post-surgery.

The surgeon uses a V-locked suture with a 2.5 cm SH needle to reconstruct the peritoneal defect with a running stitch. After treating the patent uricus located between the transverse fascia and peritoneum, the optics are repositioned 30 degrees downward to begin addressing the vesicle urico diverticulum.

The surgeon begins the surgical step by carefully exposing the surgical margins, ensuring at least one centimeter of the bladder mucosa is free of disease. A Vicryl 2-0 suture is used to ligate the base of the diverticulum in the bladder without transfixing the mucosa.

The surgeon performs a cystectomy using the tent technique, resecting the bladder component without exposing the inner layer of the intravesical mucosa. The detrusor muscle is re-approximated using a Vicryl-Trio suture with an SH-II.V needle in an extra-mucosal continuous suture.
The surgeon uses robotic optics angled thirty degrees upwards to resect two uricle cysts, starting with the first cyst located at the anterior abdominal wall, which was previously infected.
The surgeon applies the tent technique to treat the second uricle cyst located near the bladder dome, focusing on the urochle vesicle component without exposing the intravesical mucosa.

Methylene blue is used to distend the bladder, highlighting the integrity of the mucosa and differentiating it from the detrusor muscle. The tentin dissection technique is applied, involving upward traction of the bladder defect for microdissection with monopolar energy in the detrusor muscle, reaching the vesicle mucosa for ligation with generous margins. This technique avoids opening the bladder and facilitates tension-free reapproximation of the detrusor muscle.

Note that the use of this technology, how easy is to perform the bladder surgical margin in the treatment of the uric abnormalities without the need to open the bladder, thus reducing the surgical time and morbidity. With this, we hope to have managed to show how reproductible is to treat effectively and safely the abnormalities of the uricles.
The surgeon applies the tent technique to treat the uricle vesicle defect, manipulating the bladder tissue without exposing the intravesical mucosa.
